**A is incorrect:** **Aurora Serverless v2** does **not** bill at a fixed hourly rate. Unlike provisioned **Amazon RDS** instances, the ACU-based model scales and bills dynamically with actual usage, which is the core cost-optimisation benefit of the serverless model.

**C is incorrect:** Billing is based on **ACU consumption**, not the number of active connections. Even with zero connections, the database continues to run at the configured minimum capacity (at least **0.5 ACUs**) and incurs compute charges accordingly. Zero-connection billing relief requires **Aurora Serverless v1** with the auto-pause feature enabled.

**D is incorrect:** **Aurora Serverless v2** does **not** round up to whole ACUs or bill in one-hour increments. It measures capacity in fine-grained increments of **0.5 ACUs** and bills per second (with a minimum charge), making it far more granular and cost-efficient than EC2-style hourly billing.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: Aurora Serverless v2 – Scale to Zero, ACU-based Billing

text: A startup runs a development database on **Amazon Aurora MySQL** to support an internal analytics dashboard used only during business hours on weekdays (approximately 40 hours per week). The team wants to minimise database costs while retaining the ability to run ad-hoc queries at any time. They are evaluating **Aurora Serverless v2**. Which statement **BEST** describes the cost behaviour of **Aurora Serverless v2** in this use case?

options.A: **Aurora Serverless v2** scales capacity down to zero **ACUs** when idle, so the team pays nothing for compute during the ~128 hours per week when the dashboard is unused.
options.B: **Aurora Serverless v2** scales capacity to a minimum of 0.5 **ACUs** when idle rather than scaling to zero, so a small compute charge continues even when no queries are running.
options.C: **Aurora Serverless v2** bills a flat hourly instance rate regardless of actual query load, making it equivalent in cost to a provisioned **db.t3.medium** instance.
options.D: **Aurora Serverless v2** automatically pauses after 5 minutes of inactivity and resumes on the next connection, eliminating all compute charges during idle periods.

explanation:
**B is correct:** **Aurora Serverless v2** does **not** scale to zero. Its minimum capacity is **0.5 ACUs** (Aurora Capacity Units), meaning a small baseline compute charge is incurred continuously — even when no queries are executing. Teams are billed per ACU-hour consumed, which is cost-efficient under variable load, but idle periods still incur a minimal charge at the floor capacity.

**A is incorrect:** Scaling to zero ACUs (true pause/resume) is a feature of **Aurora Serverless v1**, not v2. Aurora Serverless v2 always maintains a minimum of 0.5 ACUs and does not fully suspend compute.

**C is incorrect:** **Aurora Serverless v2** does not charge a flat hourly instance rate. It uses granular ACU-based billing that scales proportionally with workload, which is fundamentally different from fixed provisioned instance pricing.

**D is incorrect:** The automatic pause-and-resume behaviour (including the 5-minute idle timeout) is characteristic of **Aurora Serverless v1**, not v2. Aurora Serverless v2 was designed for instant, fine-grained scaling without the cold-start latency associated with pausing, and it keeps capacity running at its minimum floor at all times.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: Aurora Serverless v2 – Scale to Zero, ACU-based Billing

text: A startup runs a SaaS application where each customer tenant has a dedicated **Amazon Aurora MySQL** database cluster. The application serves thousands of tenants, but any individual tenant's database is only actively queried for a few minutes each day — the rest of the time it sits completely idle. The engineering team wants to minimise database costs while ensuring each tenant's database resumes automatically when a connection arrives. They are evaluating **Aurora Serverless v2** for this workload. Which statement **MOST accurately** describes a critical limitation the team must account for when using **Aurora Serverless v2** to meet these cost goals?

options.A: **Aurora Serverless v2** cannot be used with **Amazon RDS Proxy**, so connection pooling across thousands of tenant databases is not supported.
options.B: **Aurora Serverless v2** does not support scaling below **0.5 ACUs**, meaning idle databases still incur a minimum hourly charge and cannot truly scale to zero.
options.C: **Aurora Serverless v2** requires a minimum of two **Aurora Capacity Units (ACUs)** at all times, making it more expensive than provisioned **Aurora** for always-idle workloads.
options.D: **Aurora Serverless v2** billing is based on instance-hours rather than **ACU-hours**, so per-tenant cost is identical whether the database is active or idle.

explanation:
**B is correct:** Unlike **Aurora Serverless v1**, which supports a *true scale-to-zero* capability (pausing the cluster and incurring no compute charge during inactivity), **Aurora Serverless v2** has a minimum capacity of **0.5 ACUs** and **cannot scale to zero**. An idle database always consumes at least 0.5 ACUs, which generates a continuous minimum compute charge. For a workload where thousands of tenant databases are idle for the vast majority of the day, this minimum floor accumulates into significant cost. The team should evaluate **Aurora Serverless v1** (which supports pause/resume and true scale-to-zero) or consolidate tenants onto shared clusters to control costs.

**A is incorrect:** **Aurora Serverless v2** is fully compatible with **Amazon RDS Proxy**, which is actually a recommended pattern for managing large numbers of connections to serverless clusters.

**C is incorrect:** The minimum capacity for **Aurora Serverless v2** is **0.5 ACUs**, not 2 ACUs. This is lower than stated, though it still prevents true idle cost elimination.

**D is incorrect:** **Aurora Serverless v2** billing is based on **ACU-hours** (the number of ACUs consumed multiplied by hours of use), not instance-hours. This is precisely what makes it more cost-efficient than provisioned instances for variable workloads — but the 0.5 ACU floor still applies.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: Aurora Serverless v2 – Scale to Zero, ACU-based Billing

text:
A financial services company runs a suite of internal reporting tools backed by an **Amazon Aurora MySQL** database. The workload is highly predictable: intensive analytical queries run Monday–Friday from 08:00–18:00, and the database receives **zero traffic** on evenings, weekends, and public holidays — roughly 60% of every week. The team wants to **minimise database compute costs** without changing the application connection string or managing instance lifecycle scripts. The database must be available within a few seconds when the first query arrives after an idle period. A solutions architect is comparing two options:

- **Option 1:** An **Aurora Provisioned** cluster with a `db.r6g.large` instance running 24/7.
- **Option 2:** An **Aurora Serverless v2** cluster configured with a minimum of **0.5 ACUs** and a maximum of **16 ACUs**.

Which statement **MOST accurately** describes the cost and operational trade-off between these two options for this specific workload?

options.A: **Option 1** is more cost-effective because **Aurora Provisioned** instances support Reserved Instance pricing, which can reduce the effective hourly rate by up to 60%, whereas **Aurora Serverless v2** is billed per ACU-hour with no reservation option, making it more expensive over a 1-year term.
options.B: **Option 2** is more cost-effective because **Aurora Serverless v2** scales to its minimum of 0.5 ACUs during idle periods, dramatically reducing compute charges for the ~60% of the week with no traffic, while still meeting the near-instant availability requirement — unlike **Aurora Serverless v1**, which can pause and cause cold-start delays of over a minute.
options.C: **Option 2** is more cost-effective and can fully scale to **0 ACUs** during idle periods, eliminating all compute charges on evenings and weekends, while resuming instantly when traffic arrives with no cold-start penalty.
options.D: **Option 1** is operationally simpler and more cost-effective for this workload because provisioned instances can be stopped automatically using **AWS Lambda** on a schedule via **Amazon EventBridge**, achieving the same savings as **Aurora Serverless v2** without the per-ACU overhead pricing model.

explanation:
**B is correct:** **Aurora Serverless v2** is well-suited to this workload pattern. During the ~60% of the week with zero traffic, the cluster scales down to its configured minimum — as low as **0.5 ACUs** — rather than running at full provisioned capacity. This significantly reduces compute costs compared to a 24/7 provisioned instance. Critically, **Aurora Serverless v2** does *not* fully pause (unlike **Aurora Serverless v1**); it maintains a warm, low-ACU state, so it can respond to the first incoming query within seconds rather than the 20–60+ second cold-start of v1. The application connection string remains unchanged, satisfying the no-code-change requirement.

**A is incorrect:** While **Aurora Provisioned** does support Reserved Instance pricing (1-year or 3-year terms), this discount applies to the full committed term regardless of actual utilisation. For a workload with ~60% idle time, the provisioned instance continues to incur charges at its reserved rate even when idle, whereas **Aurora Serverless v2** scales down to 0.5 ACUs during those idle periods. The variable, consumption-based billing of Serverless v2 is advantageous when a significant fraction of time is spent at very low or zero load.

**C is incorrect:** This is a critical factual error. **Aurora Serverless v2** does **NOT** scale to 0 ACUs. It scales down to the configured minimum ACU value (minimum 0.5 ACUs) but never fully pauses. The ability to scale to zero (pause) was a feature of **Aurora Serverless v1**, which also suffered from significant cold-start delays of over a minute. Stating there is 'no cold-start penalty' at 0 ACUs conflates both facts incorrectly.

**D is incorrect:** Stopping and starting a provisioned **Aurora** cluster via **Lambda** and **EventBridge** is operationally complex, introduces risk (a missed start event means application downtime), and Aurora clusters can only be stopped for a maximum of 7 days before AWS automatically restarts them. This approach does not match the seamless, automatic scaling behaviour of **Aurora Serverless v2** and introduces significant *operational overhead*, which contradicts the requirement.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: Aurora Serverless v2 – Minimum ACU Billing & Idle Cost Reduction

text: A financial services company runs a multi-tenant SaaS platform on AWS. Each tenant has a dedicated **Aurora Serverless v2** cluster to ensure data isolation. The platform currently serves 200 tenants. Usage patterns are highly asymmetric: approximately 180 tenants are inactive overnight (00:00–06:00 UTC) while the remaining 20 tenants operate in Asia-Pacific time zones and are active around the clock. The engineering team has set the minimum **Aurora Capacity Units (ACUs)** to 2 ACUs per cluster to ensure fast cold-start response times for all tenants. A cost review reveals the overnight idle compute cost is unacceptably high. The team proposes several changes. Which combination of actions will achieve the **MOST cost reduction** while maintaining acceptable response times for active Asia-Pacific tenants and tolerating a cold-start latency of up to 15 seconds for overnight-inactive tenants when they resume in the morning?

options.A: Lower the minimum ACUs to 0 for all 200 clusters, enabling **Aurora Serverless v2** to scale to zero. Accept the cold-start latency for all tenants including the 20 always-active Asia-Pacific clusters.
options.B: Lower the minimum ACUs to 0 for the 180 inactive-overnight clusters and keep the minimum ACUs at 2 for the 20 always-active Asia-Pacific clusters. Use **Amazon EventBridge Scheduler** to invoke a warm-up **AWS Lambda** function that sends a lightweight query to each inactive cluster 5 minutes before their morning peak to pre-warm the connections.
options.C: Consolidate all 200 tenants onto a single **Aurora Serverless v2** cluster using a schema-per-tenant model, set the minimum ACUs to 2 for the shared cluster, and rely on **Aurora Serverless v2** auto-scaling to handle the combined load. This eliminates per-cluster idle cost.
options.D: Migrate the 180 overnight-inactive tenant clusters to **Amazon RDS** MySQL with **db.t4g.micro** Reserved Instances (1-year, no upfront) and retain **Aurora Serverless v2** only for the 20 always-active tenants. Use **AWS Database Migration Service (DMS)** for the transition.

explanation:
**B is correct:** This solution applies targeted cost optimisation by differentiating the two tenant populations based on their actual usage patterns.

- **Scale-to-zero for 180 inactive clusters:** Setting minimum ACUs to 0 on **Aurora Serverless v2** clusters allows them to scale completely to zero during inactivity, eliminating ACU-hour charges for the overnight window. This is the primary source of savings.
- **Preserve always-on capacity for 20 active clusters:** Keeping minimum ACUs at 2 for Asia-Pacific tenants avoids any cold-start interruption for paying customers who are active 24/7.
- **EventBridge Scheduler + Lambda warm-up:** Since the business can tolerate up to 15 seconds of cold-start latency but prefers a smooth morning ramp, the pre-warm query triggered 5 minutes before the morning peak is a low-cost operational mechanism that brings clusters out of zero-scale gracefully — satisfying the constraint without keeping them warm all night.

**A is incorrect:** Scaling all 200 clusters to zero, including the 20 always-active Asia-Pacific clusters, introduces cold-start latency for live, paying customers. This violates the requirement to maintain acceptable response times for active tenants.

**C is incorrect:** Schema-per-tenant on a shared **Aurora Serverless v2** cluster breaks the data isolation requirement that is fundamental to the multi-tenant SaaS architecture described. It also introduces noisy-neighbour risks and complicates regulatory compliance.

**D is incorrect:** Migrating 180 clusters to **RDS t4g.micro** Reserved Instances incurs a fixed hourly charge 24/7, even during overnight inactivity. For clusters that are idle for 6+ hours per night, scale-to-zero on **Aurora Serverless v2** is cheaper than a running Reserved Instance. Additionally, this adds significant migration complexity and **DMS** operational overhead without guaranteeing cost savings for the overnight idle window.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: Aurora Serverless v2 – Scale to Zero, ACU-based Billing

text: A startup is launching a new mobile application that is expected to experience highly unpredictable traffic patterns, with bursts of activity at random times and periods of near-zero usage. The team wants to avoid paying for unused database capacity and does not want to manage capacity planning. Which **Amazon DynamoDB** pricing model is the **MOST cost-effective** choice for this workload?

options.A: **DynamoDB Provisioned Capacity** mode with **Auto Scaling** enabled to adjust read and write capacity units automatically
options.B: **DynamoDB On-Demand** mode, which charges only for the actual read and write requests performed
options.C: **DynamoDB Provisioned Capacity** mode with reserved capacity purchased for a 1-year term to minimise per-request costs
options.D: **DynamoDB Provisioned Capacity** mode with a fixed high throughput to ensure the application never throttles during peak bursts

explanation:
**B is correct:** **DynamoDB On-Demand** mode is designed specifically for unpredictable or spiky workloads. It charges only for the actual number of read request units (*RRUs*) and write request units (*WRUs*) consumed — there is no need to provision or manage capacity. During periods of near-zero usage, the cost drops to near zero, making it the most cost-effective option when traffic is highly variable and difficult to forecast.

**A is incorrect:** **DynamoDB Provisioned Capacity** with **Auto Scaling** can respond to traffic changes, but it scales reactively with a delay and still requires minimum provisioned capacity to be paid for at all times. It also adds operational overhead for configuring scaling policies, which the team wants to avoid.

**C is incorrect:** Purchasing reserved capacity for a 1-year term reduces per-unit costs but commits to a baseline level of capacity. This approach wastes money during the frequent low-usage periods characteristic of this workload.

**D is incorrect:** Provisioning a fixed high throughput to handle peak bursts results in significant over-provisioning and unnecessary cost during periods of low or no activity — the opposite of cost optimisation.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: DynamoDB On-demand – Unpredictable Workloads

text: A startup is launching a new mobile gaming application. User traffic is expected to be highly unpredictable — with sudden spikes during promotional events and near-zero activity during off-peak hours. The development team wants to minimise database costs and **eliminate the need to manually manage capacity**. Which **Amazon DynamoDB** pricing model is the **MOST cost-effective** choice for this workload?

options.A: Use **DynamoDB Provisioned Capacity** mode with **Auto Scaling** enabled to adjust read and write capacity units automatically
options.B: Use **DynamoDB On-Demand** mode so that the table scales instantly with actual request volume and charges only for requests made
options.C: Use **DynamoDB Provisioned Capacity** mode with reserved capacity purchased for one year to reduce the per-unit cost
options.D: Use **DynamoDB Provisioned Capacity** mode and set high fixed read/write capacity units to ensure the application never throttles during spikes

explanation:
**B is correct:** **DynamoDB On-Demand** mode is purpose-built for unpredictable or highly variable workloads. It requires **no capacity planning** — the table automatically accommodates any request volume and you are billed only for the read and write request units actually consumed. This eliminates both over-provisioning costs during idle periods and the risk of throttling during unexpected spikes, making it the most cost-effective option for this scenario.

**A is incorrect:** **Provisioned Capacity with Auto Scaling** can reduce waste, but Auto Scaling reacts to sustained load over a period of time — it cannot respond instantly to sudden, sharp spikes. The team must still set minimum and maximum capacity bounds, adding operational overhead.

**C is incorrect:** Reserved capacity requires committing to a fixed throughput level for one or three years. For a workload with near-zero off-peak traffic, paying for reserved capacity would result in significant wasted spend during quiet periods.

**D is incorrect:** Setting high fixed provisioned capacity ensures availability during spikes but is the **least cost-effective** approach — the startup would pay for maximum capacity continuously, even when the application has minimal traffic.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: DynamoDB On-demand – Unpredictable Workloads

text: A retail company runs a flash-sale platform that experiences highly unpredictable traffic spikes. During a typical week, the platform receives fewer than 50 read/write requests per second, but during promotional events it can surge to over 25,000 requests per second within minutes, with no predictable schedule. The engineering team currently uses **Amazon DynamoDB** with **Provisioned Capacity** mode. Operations staff must manually adjust capacity units before each anticipated spike, but unannounced promotions frequently cause throttling errors and degraded customer experience. The company wants to eliminate throttling, reduce operational toil, and keep database costs as low as possible during quiet periods. Which solution **MOST cost-effectively** meets these requirements?

options.A: Keep **Provisioned Capacity** mode and enable **DynamoDB Auto Scaling** with a target utilisation of 70%, setting minimum capacity to 50 RCUs/WCUs and maximum to 30,000 RCUs/WCUs
options.B: Switch to **DynamoDB On-Demand** capacity mode so the table scales instantly to any request rate and charges only for the actual read and write requests consumed
options.C: Migrate the database to **Amazon Aurora Serverless v2**, which automatically scales compute capacity and charges per **Aurora Capacity Unit (ACU)** consumed
options.D: Place **Amazon ElastiCache for Redis** in front of **DynamoDB** with **Provisioned Capacity** to absorb read traffic spikes and reduce required provisioned throughput

explanation:
**B is correct:** **DynamoDB On-Demand** capacity mode is purpose-built for workloads with unpredictable or spiky traffic patterns. It scales instantaneously to accommodate any request rate without pre-warming or manual intervention, completely eliminating throttling caused by capacity mismatches. Costs are based solely on the actual number of read and write request units consumed, meaning the company pays almost nothing during quiet periods and scales cost proportionally during spikes — with zero operational overhead for capacity management.

**A is incorrect:** **DynamoDB Auto Scaling** reacts to *sustained* utilisation changes, typically taking several minutes to scale out. This lag is insufficient for flash sales that ramp from near-zero to tens of thousands of requests per second within seconds or minutes, meaning throttling errors would still occur at the onset of an unannounced spike.

**C is incorrect:** **Amazon Aurora Serverless v2** is a relational database engine, not a key-value / document store, and would require significant application re-architecture. It also introduces higher baseline costs and does not match the use-case profile of a DynamoDB-native workload.

**D is incorrect:** While **ElastiCache** can reduce read pressure on DynamoDB, it only addresses *read* traffic and adds architectural complexity, cache-invalidation overhead, and additional cost. It does not help with write spikes and still requires the underlying DynamoDB table to be provisioned sufficiently for write traffic, leaving the core unpredictability problem unsolved.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: DynamoDB On-demand – Unpredictable Workloads

text: A fast-growing retail startup has built an e-commerce platform backed by **Amazon DynamoDB**. During normal business hours, read/write traffic averages 300 **RCUs** and 150 **WCUs**. However, the startup runs unpredictable flash sales — triggered by social media campaigns with no advance notice — during which traffic spikes to 12,000 **RCUs** and 6,000 **WCUs** for bursts lasting between 5 and 45 minutes, occurring roughly 3–4 times per month. Outside of these spikes, traffic can also drop to near-zero during overnight hours. The team has no dedicated DBA and wants to minimise manual intervention. A solutions architect is comparing **DynamoDB Provisioned Capacity** with **Auto Scaling** against **DynamoDB On-Demand** capacity mode. Which of the following statements **MOST accurately** describes the cost and operational trade-off that should drive the capacity mode decision?

options.A: **DynamoDB Provisioned Capacity with Auto Scaling** is always cheaper because **Auto Scaling** reacts to traffic increases within seconds and scales down instantly during overnight low-traffic periods, eliminating the need for **On-Demand** mode.
options.B: **DynamoDB On-Demand** is the **MOST cost-optimised** choice because it charges per request and eliminates the risk of over-provisioning during unpredictable flash sales, and the per-request price premium over provisioned capacity is justified when average utilisation is low and spikes are frequent, short-lived, and unscheduled.
options.C: **DynamoDB Provisioned Capacity with Auto Scaling** is more cost-effective because **Auto Scaling** can be configured with a target utilisation of 70% and a minimum capacity of 300 **RCUs** / 150 **WCUs**, which will absorb all flash-sale spikes before **On-Demand** billing kicks in.
options.D: **DynamoDB On-Demand** should only be selected when the workload is completely uniform throughout the day; for any workload with identifiable peak windows, **Provisioned Capacity with Auto Scaling** is always the correct choice to avoid the higher per-request cost.

explanation:
**B is correct:** **DynamoDB On-Demand** is purpose-built for workloads that are *genuinely unpredictable* — spikes triggered without notice (e.g., viral social media campaigns) that cannot be pre-scheduled or anticipated. Key reasons On-Demand is optimal here: (1) Flash sales are unscheduled and cannot be pre-announced to Auto Scaling; (2) Average utilisation is very low (near-zero overnight), so provisioned capacity would be mostly idle outside spikes; (3) The team has no DBA, so operational simplicity is a hard requirement; (4) On-Demand charges only for actual reads/writes consumed, meaning the per-request premium is economically justified when you would otherwise over-provision to handle infrequent but extreme spikes. The total monthly cost of paying ~2.5–3× the per-RCU/WCU rate on-demand is typically lower than permanently provisioning or slowly auto-scaling to 12,000 RCUs and 6,000 WCUs for only 3–4 events per month.

**A is incorrect:** **Auto Scaling** does NOT react within seconds. Scale-out events use **CloudWatch** alarms with a default evaluation period of 2 minutes, and the scaling action itself adds additional latency. For a spike that ramps up instantaneously from a social media post, Auto Scaling will throttle requests until capacity is provisioned, causing degraded customer experience. Scale-in is also gradual and governed by cooldown periods — it does not 'instantly' drop capacity overnight.

**C is incorrect:** Setting a minimum provisioned capacity of 300 **RCUs** / 150 **WCUs** with 70% target utilisation does NOT absorb flash-sale peaks. Auto Scaling would need several minutes to ramp from 300 to 12,000 RCUs in response to a sudden spike, during which requests are throttled. Moreover, maintaining even minimum provisioned capacity 24/7 incurs cost during near-zero overnight periods, unlike On-Demand which charges $0 when no requests are made.

**D is incorrect:** This statement inverts the correct guidance. **AWS best practice** recommends On-Demand precisely for workloads that have *unpredictable* peaks — even if some peaks are loosely correlated with business events. On-Demand is not restricted to flat, uniform workloads. Provisioned capacity with Auto Scaling is preferred when traffic patterns are *predictable and gradual*, allowing pre-warming or scheduled scaling actions.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: DynamoDB On-demand – Unpredictable Workloads

text: A retail company runs a global e-commerce platform backed by **Amazon DynamoDB**. The platform experiences highly unpredictable traffic: during off-peak periods, the table receives fewer than 10 requests per second, but during flash sales and holiday events — which occur roughly 8–10 times per year and last between 2 and 6 hours — traffic can spike to over 50,000 requests per second with no advance warning. The company currently uses **DynamoDB Provisioned Capacity** with **Auto Scaling** configured at 70% target utilisation. The operations team reports that during sudden spikes, Auto Scaling cannot react fast enough, causing throttled requests and failed orders. Finance has flagged that provisioned capacity is significantly over-provisioned between events to provide a safety buffer, costing the company an estimated $14,000/month in idle capacity. A solutions architect must redesign the capacity strategy to eliminate throttling, remove the idle-capacity cost, and minimise operational overhead — without re-architecting the application layer or introducing caching. Which combination of actions meets ALL of these requirements?

options.A: Switch the table to **DynamoDB On-Demand** capacity mode for all tables. Remove all **Auto Scaling** policies. Accept that on-demand pricing per request-unit is higher than provisioned pricing, and use **AWS Cost Explorer** reserved-capacity recommendations to convert back to provisioned capacity after 30 days of on-demand data is available.
options.B: Switch the table to **DynamoDB On-Demand** capacity mode. Remove all **Auto Scaling** policies. Enable **DynamoDB Global Tables** to distribute read traffic across two **AWS Regions**, halving per-region request volume and reducing the effective on-demand cost during spikes.
options.C: Switch the table to **DynamoDB On-Demand** capacity mode. Remove all **Auto Scaling** policies. Monitor monthly spend using **AWS Cost Explorer** and evaluate switching back to **Provisioned Capacity with Auto Scaling** if the workload stabilises into a predictable pattern — keeping on-demand as the long-term default for the current unpredictable workload.
options.D: Keep **DynamoDB Provisioned Capacity** but increase the **Auto Scaling** maximum to 100,000 RCU/WCU and reduce the scale-in cooldown period to 60 seconds. Pair this with an **Amazon SQS** queue in front of writes to absorb burst traffic and smooth the write rate seen by DynamoDB, eliminating throttling without migrating capacity modes.

explanation:
**C is correct:** **DynamoDB On-Demand** capacity mode instantly accommodates any request volume without pre-provisioning or manual scaling — it scales up and down automatically with zero throttling and zero idle-capacity cost. Removing **Auto Scaling** policies eliminates the misconfigured safety buffer that was costing $14,000/month in idle capacity. Because on-demand charges are purely per request-unit consumed, the company pays only for actual traffic. Using **AWS Cost Explorer** to evaluate a future switch back to provisioned capacity is the standard AWS cost-optimisation lifecycle: collect on-demand usage data, identify stable baseline traffic, then switch the predictable baseline portion to provisioned (or reserved) capacity for savings — while retaining on-demand for the unpredictable spike component if needed. This fully satisfies all three constraints: no throttling, no idle cost, minimal operational overhead, and no application-layer changes.

**A is incorrect:** Switching to on-demand is correct, but the suggestion to use **Cost Explorer reserved-capacity recommendations** after 30 days and convert *all* capacity back to provisioned undermines the stated goal. The workload is explicitly unpredictable year-round; converting back to provisioned would reintroduce both the throttling risk and the idle-buffer cost problem. On-demand is the correct *long-term* default here, not a 30-day stopgap.

**B is incorrect:** Enabling **DynamoDB Global Tables** to spread read load across two regions does not reduce cost — it *multiplies* cost because Global Tables replicate all writes to every region, roughly doubling write-unit charges. The question explicitly states no re-architecting of the application layer, and multi-region replication is a significant architectural change that adds complexity, cross-region replication costs, and latency considerations without solving the core throttling or idle-capacity problem.

**D is incorrect:** Keeping provisioned capacity with an increased Auto Scaling maximum and reduced cooldown does not solve the root problem: **Auto Scaling reacts to CloudWatch metrics with a lag of 1–3 minutes**, which is insufficient for instantaneous flash-sale spikes to 50,000 RPS. The **Amazon SQS** buffer for writes introduces application-layer changes (a queue abstraction the application must write to), violates the constraint of not re-architecting the application layer, and still does nothing to protect read traffic — which is also part of a flash-sale workload.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: DynamoDB On-demand – Unpredictable Workloads

text: A company runs a high-traffic e-commerce web application backed by **Amazon RDS**. The database is experiencing heavy read load because the application retrieves the same session data on almost every request. A solutions architect wants to reduce the load on **Amazon RDS** in the **MOST cost-effective** way. Which approach should the solutions architect recommend?

options.A: Add **Read Replicas** to **Amazon RDS** to distribute the session data read traffic across multiple database instances
options.B: Migrate the application to **Amazon DynamoDB** to handle session data at scale
options.C: Deploy **Amazon ElastiCache** to store and serve frequently accessed session data in-memory, reducing repeated reads to **Amazon RDS**
options.D: Increase the instance size of the **Amazon RDS** instance to handle the additional session read load

explanation:
**C is correct:** **Amazon ElastiCache** is a fully managed, in-memory caching service designed to offload repetitive read workloads — such as session data — from relational databases. Because session data is read on nearly every request, caching it in **ElastiCache** (using **Redis** or **Memcached**) dramatically reduces the number of queries hitting **Amazon RDS**, lowering both database load and cost.

**A is incorrect:** **RDS Read Replicas** distribute read traffic but do not eliminate redundant reads — the database still processes every session query. Read Replicas also incur additional instance costs without caching benefit.

**B is incorrect:** Migrating the entire database layer to **Amazon DynamoDB** is a significant architectural change and introduces migration risk and effort. It is not the **MOST cost-effective** or simplest solution for the stated problem of session offloading.

**D is incorrect:** Scaling up the **RDS** instance (vertical scaling) increases cost without addressing the root cause — the same session data being read repeatedly. It is neither cost-effective nor architecturally sound compared to caching.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: ElastiCache for Session Offloading – Reduce DB Load

text: A company runs a high-traffic e-commerce web application backed by **Amazon RDS for MySQL**. During peak hours, the application performs thousands of repeated session-state lookups per second, causing the **RDS** instance CPU utilisation to spike above 90% and increasing response latency. The engineering team wants to reduce database load and improve application performance with the **LEAST operational overhead**. Which solution **BEST** meets these requirements?

options.A: Increase the **RDS** instance size to a memory-optimised instance type to handle the additional read throughput
options.B: Deploy **Amazon ElastiCache for Redis** to store and serve session-state data, offloading repeated lookups from **Amazon RDS**
options.C: Enable **RDS Read Replicas** and direct all session-state read queries to the replica endpoints
options.D: Use **Amazon DynamoDB** as the primary data store and migrate all session data away from **Amazon RDS**

explanation:
**B is correct:** **Amazon ElastiCache for Redis** is purpose-built for caching frequently accessed, short-lived data such as session state. By storing session-state data in an in-memory cache, the application retrieves session information directly from **ElastiCache** rather than querying **Amazon RDS**, dramatically reducing database CPU pressure and read latency. This is a well-established *cost-optimisation* pattern — a smaller (and cheaper) **RDS** instance can then handle transactional workloads efficiently. **ElastiCache** is fully managed, requiring minimal operational overhead to configure and maintain.

**A is incorrect:** Scaling up the **RDS** instance type addresses the symptom (high CPU) but not the root cause (redundant, repeated session lookups hitting the database). This is also **more expensive** and provides no architectural improvement — it is not the most cost-effective or operationally optimal approach.

**C is incorrect:** **RDS Read Replicas** are appropriate for scaling *analytical or reporting read workloads*, not for caching session-state data. Each session lookup would still hit the database replica, doing nothing to reduce overall DB load or cost. Additionally, **Read Replicas** introduce replication lag and added complexity.

**D is incorrect:** Migrating all session data to **Amazon DynamoDB** could work technically, but it requires significant application refactoring and database migration effort, resulting in **high operational overhead**. It is not the recommended pattern when a caching layer such as **ElastiCache** can solve the problem with minimal changes.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: ElastiCache for Session Offloading – Reduce DB Load

text: A company runs a high-traffic e-commerce application backed by **Amazon RDS for MySQL** (Multi-AZ). During peak sales events, the database CPU regularly exceeds 85% and response times degrade noticeably. A detailed analysis shows that 65% of all database queries are repeated read operations fetching the same user **session data** within a 30-minute window. The engineering team wants to reduce database load and improve read performance with **MINIMAL changes to application code** and the **LOWEST ongoing operational overhead**. Which solution BEST meets these requirements?

options.A: Deploy **Amazon ElastiCache for Redis** in front of **Amazon RDS**. Cache user session data with a **TTL** of 30 minutes and update the application to check the cache before querying the database.
options.B: Add **RDS Read Replicas** and distribute all read traffic across the replicas using a **custom load balancer** built on **Amazon EC2**.
options.C: Migrate the application to use **Amazon DynamoDB** as the primary data store, leveraging its built-in horizontal scaling to handle peak traffic.
options.D: Enable **RDS Proxy** and increase the **RDS instance class** to the next tier to absorb the additional concurrent connection load during peak events.

explanation:
**A is correct:** **Amazon ElastiCache for Redis** is purpose-built for exactly this use case — caching frequently repeated, short-lived data such as user session objects. By adding a cache layer with a **TTL** matching the 30-minute reuse window, the application serves 65% of reads from in-memory cache rather than hitting **Amazon RDS**, directly reducing CPU pressure. The code change is minimal (check cache → on miss, query DB and populate cache). ElastiCache is a fully managed service, so ongoing operational overhead is low.

**B is incorrect:** **RDS Read Replicas** can distribute reads, but a custom load balancer on **Amazon EC2** introduces significant operational overhead (patching, scaling, failover logic). It also doesn't eliminate redundant queries — the same session data is still fetched from disk on every request.

**C is incorrect:** Migrating to **Amazon DynamoDB** would require substantial application re-architecture and data migration effort, directly violating the *minimal code change* constraint. It also does not address the root cause — repeated reads of identical data within a short window.

**D is incorrect:** **RDS Proxy** improves connection pooling and reduces connection overhead, but it does not cache query results or reduce the number of queries reaching the database. Upsizing the RDS instance addresses symptoms rather than the root cause and increases cost without eliminating redundant reads.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: ElastiCache for Session Offloading – Reduce DB Load

text:
A rapidly growing e-commerce company runs a multi-tier web application on **Amazon EC2** instances backed by **Amazon Aurora MySQL**. The application manages user sessions in the database, and the solutions architect has observed that 73% of all database queries are session read/write operations. During peak traffic events, the **Aurora** cluster's CPU consistently spikes above 95%, causing latency degradation and occasional timeouts. The database tier costs $4,200/month. The engineering team has proposed four architectural changes. The solution must reduce database load and overall costs without requiring application re-architecture or changing the session management interface.

Which solution **MOST cost-effectively** reduces the database load caused by session management?

options.A: Add two additional **Aurora Read Replicas** to horizontally scale read capacity, and configure the application to route all session reads to the reader endpoint and session writes to the writer endpoint.
options.B: Introduce an **Amazon ElastiCache for Redis** cluster in front of **Aurora**, store all user sessions exclusively in **ElastiCache**, and configure session TTL to expire idle sessions automatically.
options.C: Migrate the session data to a separate **Amazon DynamoDB** table with **on-demand capacity mode**, and configure the application to query **DynamoDB** for session lookups instead of **Aurora**.
options.D: Enable **Aurora Auto Scaling** to add read replicas dynamically during peak periods, and configure **Aurora Serverless v2** for the writer instance to scale compute automatically.

explanation:
**B is correct:** Since 73% of all database queries are session operations, offloading session storage entirely to **Amazon ElastiCache for Redis** directly eliminates that workload from **Aurora**. **ElastiCache** operates as an in-memory data store with sub-millisecond latency, is purpose-built for ephemeral session data, and supports native TTL-based expiry — which maps directly to session lifecycle management without any change to the session management interface. The cost of a small **ElastiCache** cluster is a fraction of scaling the **Aurora** cluster, and removing 73% of query volume from **Aurora** would allow right-sizing the database tier, reducing the $4,200/month cost significantly. This is the textbook *offload-to-cache* pattern for session management.

**A is incorrect:** Adding **Aurora Read Replicas** increases costs proportionally to the number of replicas added, and each replica costs nearly as much as the primary instance. More critically, session *write* operations (which Aurora Read Replicas cannot serve) remain on the writer and still contribute to CPU pressure. This does not reduce overall cost and only partially addresses reads.

**C is incorrect:** While **Amazon DynamoDB** is a valid key-value store for sessions, migrating to DynamoDB *does* require an application-layer change — the data access layer must be redirected from the Aurora JDBC/MySQL driver to the DynamoDB SDK or API. This violates the constraint of not changing the session management interface. Additionally, DynamoDB on-demand pricing at high request volumes can exceed ElastiCache costs.

**D is incorrect:** **Aurora Auto Scaling** and **Aurora Serverless v2** address compute scaling but do not reduce the number of queries hitting the database. The session query load remains on Aurora regardless of how the compute scales. This approach increases cost during peaks (due to ACU scaling charges) and does not solve the root cause — session data polluting the relational database workload.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: ElastiCache for Session Offloading – Reduce DB Load

text: A high-traffic e-commerce platform runs a multi-tier application across three **Availability Zones**. The application tier consists of stateless **EC2** instances managed by an **Auto Scaling group** behind an **Application Load Balancer**. Session state is currently stored in an **Amazon RDS for MySQL** Multi-AZ instance. During peak sales events, database CPU consistently spikes to 92%, query latency exceeds 800 ms, and the team is forced to vertically scale the **RDS** instance — increasing costs significantly. A solutions architect reviews the metrics and finds that 78% of all database queries are session-state reads, and average session lifetime is 35 minutes. The remaining 22% are transactional writes that must be durable. The team has the following additional constraints: (1) session data must survive an individual **EC2** instance failure, (2) the solution must not require application code changes beyond updating a connection endpoint, (3) the cost increase from any new service must be offset by the ability to downscale the **RDS** instance, and (4) the architecture must tolerate the failure of a single **Availability Zone** without session loss. Which combination of actions meets ALL four constraints with the **MOST cost-effective** and **LEAST operationally complex** architecture?

options.A: Deploy an **Amazon ElastiCache for Redis** cluster in **Cluster Mode Disabled** with Multi-AZ replication across three **Availability Zones**. Configure the application to store and retrieve session state from **ElastiCache** using the existing Redis session handler, pointing to the **ElastiCache** primary endpoint. Downscale the **RDS** instance to the next smaller instance class. Retain **RDS** Multi-AZ for transactional writes only.
options.B: Deploy an **Amazon ElastiCache for Memcached** cluster with three nodes — one per **Availability Zone**. Update the application session handler to distribute session keys across all three nodes using consistent hashing. Downscale the **RDS** instance to a single-AZ deployment to further reduce costs. Enable **RDS** automated backups for durability of transactional writes.
options.C: Replace **Amazon RDS** with **Amazon DynamoDB** for both session storage and transactional writes. Enable **DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX)** for read caching. Configure a **DynamoDB** global table across three **Availability Zones** to meet the AZ-failure tolerance constraint. Decommission **RDS** entirely to eliminate its cost.
options.D: Deploy an **Amazon ElastiCache for Redis** cluster in **Cluster Mode Disabled** with a single replica node in the same **Availability Zone** as the primary. Store session data in **ElastiCache** and offload all session reads from **RDS**. Downscale **RDS** to a smaller instance class. Enable **ElastiCache** automatic failover to meet the AZ-failure tolerance requirement.

explanation:
**A is correct:** This option satisfies all four constraints simultaneously.

1. **Session survival across EC2 failure:** By storing session state in **ElastiCache for Redis** (external to the instance), any individual **EC2** instance failure has no impact on session data — a replacement instance retrieves the same session from **ElastiCache**.

2. **No application code changes beyond endpoint update:** **ElastiCache for Redis** is protocol-compatible with open-source Redis. Standard Redis session handlers (PHP, Java, Node.js, etc.) require only a connection string change — no code refactoring.

3. **Cost offset via RDS downscale:** Offloading 78% of queries (all session reads) dramatically reduces **RDS** CPU and IOPS. The **RDS** instance can be safely downscaled, and the savings will typically offset or exceed the cost of the **ElastiCache** cluster.

4. **AZ-failure tolerance without session loss:** **ElastiCache for Redis** in **Cluster Mode Disabled** with Multi-AZ replication enabled synchronously replicates to a read replica in a different **AZ**. Automatic failover promotes the replica if the primary **AZ** fails, preserving all session data.

**B is incorrect:** **Memcached** does not support replication. A node failure (or AZ failure) causes session loss for all sessions pinned to that node, violating constraints 1 and 4. Additionally, disabling **RDS** Multi-AZ removes durability for transactional writes, violating constraint 3's implied durability requirement.

**C is incorrect:** Replacing **RDS** with **DynamoDB** for transactional writes is a significant architectural and schema migration requiring extensive application code changes — violating constraint 2. **DynamoDB Global Tables** are also a cross-Region feature, not an intra-Region Multi-AZ construct, making this architecturally incorrect for the stated AZ-failure requirement.

**D is incorrect:** Placing the **ElastiCache** primary and its single replica in the **same Availability Zone** does not provide AZ-level resilience. If that **AZ** fails, both the primary and replica are lost simultaneously, violating constraint 4. Multi-AZ requires the replica to reside in a *different* **AZ**.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: ElastiCache for Session Offloading – Reduce DB Load

text: A company runs a web application on **Amazon EC2** instances. The application reads large datasets from an **Amazon RDS** database. A solutions architect wants to **minimise data transfer costs**. In which scenario does AWS charge **NO data transfer fee** between the **EC2** instance and the **RDS** instance?

options.A: The **EC2** instance and **RDS** instance are in different **Availability Zones** within the same **AWS Region**
options.B: The **EC2** instance and **RDS** instance are in the **same Availability Zone** within the same **AWS Region**
options.C: The **EC2** instance is in one **AWS Region** and the **RDS** instance is in a different **AWS Region**
options.D: The **EC2** instance communicates with **RDS** through an **Internet Gateway** in the same **VPC**

explanation:
**B is correct:** Data transfer between **EC2** and **RDS** (or any two AWS resources) within the **same Availability Zone** is **free of charge**. AWS does not apply data transfer fees when traffic stays within a single AZ.

**A is incorrect:** When **EC2** and **RDS** are in **different Availability Zones** within the same Region, AWS charges a cross-AZ data transfer fee (typically $0.01 per GB in each direction). This is a common cost trap in Multi-AZ or read-replica architectures.

**C is incorrect:** Cross-region data transfer incurs the highest intra-AWS data transfer costs, charged at per-GB rates that vary by Region pair.

**D is incorrect:** Routing traffic through an **Internet Gateway** introduces internet data transfer charges for outbound traffic, and does not eliminate fees — it adds them. Traffic should stay within the **VPC** and same AZ to avoid charges entirely.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: Data Transfer Costs – Same AZ vs Cross-AZ vs Cross-region vs Internet

text: A solutions architect is designing a data pipeline on AWS. An application running on an **Amazon EC2** instance in **us-east-1a** needs to read large volumes of data from an **Amazon RDS** MySQL database. The architect is evaluating three placement options for the **RDS** instance to minimise data transfer costs. Which statement **BEST** describes the data transfer pricing behaviour across these options?

options.A: Data transfer between an **EC2** instance and an **RDS** instance is free when both are in the same **AWS Region**, regardless of whether they are in the same or different **Availability Zones**.
options.B: Data transfer between an **EC2** instance and an **RDS** instance in the **same Availability Zone** using private IP addresses is free, while transfer across **Availability Zones** within the same **Region** incurs a per-GB charge in each direction.
options.C: Data transfer between an **EC2** instance and an **RDS** instance is always free within the same **VPC**, regardless of **Availability Zone** placement or whether public or private IP addresses are used.
options.D: Data transfer between an **EC2** instance and an **RDS** instance across **Availability Zones** is free, but transferring data out to the internet incurs a charge only after the first 100 GB per month.

explanation:
**B is correct:** AWS charges **$0.01 per GB in each direction** for data transferred between **EC2** (or **RDS**) instances across different **Availability Zones** within the same Region when using private IP addresses. When both resources reside in the **same Availability Zone** and communicate via private IP, there is **no data transfer charge**. This makes same-AZ placement the *most cost-effective* option for high-throughput workloads, although it does reduce availability.

**A is incorrect:** Being in the same Region alone is **not** sufficient to avoid data transfer costs. Cross-AZ traffic within a Region is still billable at $0.01/GB per direction.

**C is incorrect:** **VPC** membership does not override data transfer pricing. Cross-AZ charges apply regardless of whether instances share the same **VPC**, and using public IP addresses for intra-Region traffic actually incurs higher charges than private IP.

**D is incorrect:** Cross-AZ data transfer is **not free** — it incurs a per-GB charge. The statement about internet egress pricing is also imprecise; the free tier applies only to the first 100 GB per month for **new accounts**, not as a general ongoing exemption.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: Data Transfer Costs – Same AZ vs Cross-AZ vs Cross-region vs Internet

text: A company runs a three-tier web application in **AWS**. The application tier consists of **Amazon EC2** instances in an **Auto Scaling group** spread across two **Availability Zones** (AZ-1 and AZ-2) in the **us-east-1** region. The data tier is a **Multi-AZ Amazon RDS for MySQL** instance with the primary in AZ-1 and the standby in AZ-2. The application instances in both AZs query the RDS **primary instance** directly using its DNS endpoint. A solutions architect is reviewing the monthly bill and notices significant data transfer charges. The application processes approximately **500 GB** of query results per day returned from the database to the application tier. Which change would **MOST** reduce the data transfer costs while maintaining high availability?

options.A: Enable **Multi-AZ** on the **RDS** instance and configure the application to use the **RDS read replica endpoint** in each AZ so that instances in AZ-2 read from the standby replica locally
options.B: Deploy an **Amazon ElastiCache for Memcached** cluster in each AZ and configure the application to cache frequent query results locally, reducing round-trips to **RDS**
options.C: Create an **RDS Read Replica** in AZ-2 and configure application instances in AZ-2 to query the read replica, so that cross-AZ data transfer for read traffic is eliminated
options.D: Migrate the database to **Amazon Aurora Global Database** so that read traffic can be served from a local region replica, eliminating all cross-AZ transfer charges

explanation:
**C is correct:** Data transferred between **EC2** instances and **RDS** across different **Availability Zones** is charged at **$0.01/GB** per direction. With **500 GB/day** of query results flowing from the primary in AZ-1 to application instances in AZ-2, this cross-AZ transfer cost is substantial. Creating an **RDS Read Replica** in AZ-2 and directing read traffic from AZ-2 application instances to that local replica means the data stays within AZ-2 — data transfer between resources *within the same AZ* is **free**. This directly eliminates the cross-AZ transfer cost on read traffic, which constitutes the majority of the workload, while the **Multi-AZ primary** continues to provide high availability for writes.

**A is incorrect:** The **Multi-AZ standby** instance is not accessible as a read endpoint — it exists solely for automatic failover. AWS does not expose a queryable endpoint for the standby replica in a standard **Multi-AZ RDS** deployment. This option is architecturally invalid.

**B is incorrect:** **ElastiCache** can reduce the *number* of database queries, which may indirectly lower data transfer, but it does not eliminate cross-AZ transfer for cache misses or for data that cannot be effectively cached. It also introduces additional cost (ElastiCache node fees) and operational overhead without directly addressing the root cause — the cross-AZ topology.

**D is incorrect:** **Amazon Aurora Global Database** is designed to replicate data across AWS **regions** to reduce cross-region read latency. It does not address intra-region cross-AZ data transfer costs and would introduce unnecessary complexity and expense for a single-region use case.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: Data Transfer Costs – Same AZ vs Cross-AZ vs Cross-region vs Internet

text: A media company runs a video transcoding pipeline on **AWS** in **us-east-1**. The pipeline has three tiers: (1) an ingestion layer that receives raw video from external clients over the internet, (2) a transcoding fleet of **EC2** instances deployed in **private subnets** across **two Availability Zones**, which currently reach **Amazon S3** via a **NAT Gateway**, and (3) a delivery layer that pushes finished video to viewers globally. The team has measured the following monthly data flows: **40 TB** of raw video uploaded by external clients; **200 TB** of intermediate files read and written between the transcoding **EC2** instances and **Amazon S3**; **600 TB** of finished video served to end users. Currently, end-user delivery is handled by direct **S3** presigned URLs (standard S3 internet egress). A solutions architect is tasked with redesigning the architecture to **MOST significantly reduce data transfer costs** without changing functional behaviour. Which combination of changes addresses the **two largest cost drivers**?

options.A: Add a **Gateway VPC Endpoint** for **Amazon S3** to eliminate **NAT Gateway** data-processing charges on the 200 TB EC2↔S3 flow, and front end-user delivery with **Amazon CloudFront** to reduce internet egress costs on the 600 TB delivery flow
options.B: Migrate the transcoding **EC2** instances from private subnets to public subnets to bypass **NAT Gateway** charges, and continue using direct **S3** presigned URLs for end-user delivery
options.C: Add a **Gateway VPC Endpoint** for **Amazon S3**, and replicate the **S3** bucket to every **AWS Region** closest to end users to reduce delivery latency and egress costs
options.D: Front end-user delivery with **Amazon CloudFront**, and keep the existing **NAT Gateway** path for EC2↔S3 traffic because **NAT Gateway** data-processing fees are negligible at 200 TB scale

explanation:
**A is correct:** This option targets the two genuine and largest cost drivers in the described architecture.

**Cost driver 1 — NAT Gateway data-processing charges (200 TB/month):** EC2 instances in private subnets currently reach **Amazon S3** via a **NAT Gateway**. **NAT Gateway** charges **$0.045 per GB** for data processing in addition to per-hour charges. At 200 TB/month, this amounts to roughly **$9,000/month** in data-processing fees alone. A **Gateway VPC Endpoint** for **S3** routes EC2↔S3 traffic directly within the **AWS** network, completely **bypassing the NAT Gateway**. The endpoint itself is **free to create and use** — there are no per-GB charges — making this the single largest saving. Note: EC2-to-S3 data transfer within the same AWS Region is already free at the S3 level; the cost being eliminated here is the **NAT Gateway processing fee**, not an S3 transfer charge.

**Cost driver 2 — Internet egress for end-user delivery (600 TB/month):** Standard **S3** internet egress is charged at approximately **$0.09/GB** (first 10 TB) stepping down to **$0.07/GB** for higher tiers. **Amazon CloudFront** *Data Transfer Out to Internet* rates are lower (from **$0.085/GB** stepping to **$0.02/GB** at high volumes), and **CloudFront-to-S3 origin fetches** within the same Region are **free**. At 600 TB/month, switching from direct S3 presigned URL egress to **CloudFront** delivery yields substantial per-GB savings at scale, in addition to caching benefits that reduce origin requests.

**B is incorrect:** Moving EC2 instances to public subnets removes **NAT Gateway** charges but introduces significant security risks (direct internet exposure of transcoding instances) and is not an architecturally sound solution. It also does nothing to reduce the 600 TB delivery cost.

**C is incorrect:** Replicating the **S3** bucket across multiple Regions incurs **S3 Cross-Region Replication** data transfer charges and ongoing storage costs in every Region, which would **increase** total costs rather than reduce them. **CloudFront** already solves global delivery cost-effectively from a single origin.

**D is incorrect:** At 200 TB/month, **NAT Gateway** data-processing fees are far from negligible — approximately $9,000/month. Retaining the **NAT Gateway** path while only adding **CloudFront** addresses only one of the two major cost drivers and leaves the largest single cost unreduced.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: Data Transfer Costs – Same AZ vs Cross-AZ vs Cross-region vs Internet

text:
A company runs a three-tier e-commerce platform entirely in **us-east-1**. The architecture consists of: (1) an **Application Load Balancer** in a public subnet, (2) a fleet of **EC2** web/app instances in *private subnets* spread across three **Availability Zones** — all outbound internet traffic routes through a **NAT Gateway**, and (3) a **Multi-AZ Amazon RDS for MySQL** cluster. A data pipeline job runs nightly and performs the following operations:

- Pulls **180 GB** of raw event data from an **Amazon S3** bucket in **us-east-1** into the EC2 app tier for transformation.
- Writes **60 GB** of transformed results back to **Amazon S3** in **us-east-1**.
- Replicates **25 GB** of aggregated reports to an **Amazon S3** bucket in **eu-west-1** for a European analytics team.
- The app-tier EC2 instances query the **RDS primary** instance heavily during transformation; the **RDS standby** sits in a different AZ.

The solutions architect has identified data transfer and NAT Gateway processing costs as the dominant cost drivers. Which combination of changes would MOST significantly reduce monthly data transfer costs while maintaining availability and data consistency?

options.A: Deploy a **VPC Gateway Endpoint** for **Amazon S3** so that EC2-to-S3 traffic bypasses the **NAT Gateway**; read from an **RDS read replica** in the same AZ as the EC2 instances instead of the primary to avoid cross-AZ charges; and compress the cross-region replication payload before sending to **eu-west-1**.
options.B: Deploy a **VPC Gateway Endpoint** for **Amazon S3** to eliminate **NAT Gateway data-processing charges** on EC2-to-S3 traffic; pin the nightly job's EC2 instances to the **same AZ as the RDS primary** to avoid cross-AZ RDS query traffic charges; and use **Amazon S3 Cross-Region Replication (CRR)** triggered from S3 rather than copying from EC2, removing the EC2-driven egress component for the 25 GB to **eu-west-1**.
options.C: Replace the **Multi-AZ RDS** deployment with a **Single-AZ RDS** instance to eliminate standby replication costs; use **S3 Transfer Acceleration** for cross-region report delivery to **eu-west-1**; and move the EC2 app instances to a public subnet to avoid NAT Gateway charges.
options.D: Use **AWS Direct Connect** to route all S3 traffic and eliminate NAT Gateway fees; migrate the nightly job to **AWS Lambda** to avoid EC2 data transfer billing; and schedule the cross-region replication during AWS free-tier off-peak windows.

explanation:
**B is correct — analysis of each cost lever:**

**1. VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3 (largest saving):** Because the EC2 instances are in *private subnets*, all traffic to S3 public endpoints currently passes through the **NAT Gateway**, which charges a **data-processing fee of ~$0.045/GB** in each direction. A **VPC Gateway Endpoint** routes EC2-to-S3 traffic over the AWS private backbone, completely bypassing the NAT Gateway. With 180 GB inbound + 60 GB outbound = 240 GB/night, avoiding the NAT Gateway processing charge represents the single largest cost reduction. Note: EC2-to-S3 data transfer within the same Region is $0.00 regardless of routing path — the saving here is specifically the **NAT Gateway data-processing charge**, not an S3 data transfer fee.

**2. Pinning EC2 to the same AZ as the RDS primary:** Cross-AZ data transfer is billed at **$0.01/GB in each direction**. The nightly job issues heavy read/write queries against the RDS primary. If the EC2 instances are in a *different AZ*, every query response byte incurs cross-AZ charges. Placing the job instances in the **same AZ as the RDS primary** reduces this to zero. Multi-AZ standby replication is an internal RDS mechanism with no separate customer-visible charge, so availability is fully preserved.

**3. S3 CRR instead of EC2-driven copy to eu-west-1:** Cross-region data transfer charges are unavoidable for the 25 GB replicated to **eu-west-1** (~$0.02/GB). However, if EC2 drives the copy, the data traverses the NAT Gateway *again* on the way out, adding NAT Gateway processing charges on top of cross-region fees. Using **S3 Cross-Region Replication** keeps the transfer entirely within the S3 service layer, eliminating the additional NAT Gateway egress component.

**Why A is incorrect:** Reading from an **RDS read replica** in the same AZ is a valid cross-AZ cost-saving technique, but a read replica introduces *replication lag* and cannot guarantee that the transformation job sees the latest committed writes — violating the data consistency requirement. Compression reduces payload size but is not an AWS billing mechanism and does not eliminate the underlying per-GB cross-region transfer charge.

**Why C is incorrect:** AWS does **not** charge customers separately for Multi-AZ RDS standby replication — it is included in the Multi-AZ instance price. Eliminating Multi-AZ destroys high availability with zero cost benefit. **S3 Transfer Acceleration** *increases* per-GB cost and is designed for upload speed improvements, not cost reduction. Moving instances to public subnets introduces security risk and does not eliminate data transfer charges.

**Why D is incorrect:** **AWS Direct Connect** carries significant fixed port-hour charges and long provisioning timelines; it is not cost-effective for a nightly batch workload. **Lambda** does not eliminate data transfer billing — data transferred through Lambda is billed identically to EC2 egress. There are no documented 'off-peak free windows' for cross-region S3 replication.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: Data Transfer Costs – Same AZ vs Cross-AZ vs Cross-region vs Internet

text: A company runs several **Amazon EC2** instances in a private subnet that frequently read and write large amounts of data to **Amazon S3** and **Amazon DynamoDB**. All outbound internet traffic currently flows through a **NAT Gateway**, resulting in unexpectedly high data-processing charges. A solutions architect wants to reduce costs **without** changing the application code or introducing additional operational complexity. Which solution **MOST cost-effectively** eliminates the **NAT Gateway** data-processing charges for this traffic?

options.A: Move the **EC2** instances to a public subnet and assign them **Elastic IP** addresses so they connect directly to **Amazon S3** and **Amazon DynamoDB**
options.B: Create **Gateway VPC Endpoints** for **Amazon S3** and **Amazon DynamoDB** and update the route tables for the private subnet to route traffic through these endpoints
options.C: Deploy an **Application Load Balancer** in front of the **EC2** instances and configure it to route **Amazon S3** and **Amazon DynamoDB** traffic internally
options.D: Enable **VPC Flow Logs** to identify high-cost traffic patterns, then use **AWS Cost Explorer** to request a NAT Gateway usage discount

explanation:
**B is correct:** **Gateway VPC Endpoints** for **Amazon S3** and **Amazon DynamoDB** allow traffic from private subnets to reach these services entirely within the AWS network — without passing through a **NAT Gateway**. Because the traffic no longer traverses the **NAT Gateway**, the per-GB data-processing charges are eliminated. **Gateway VPC Endpoints** are available at no additional cost, making this the **MOST cost-effective** solution with minimal operational overhead.

**A is incorrect:** Moving instances to a public subnet and assigning **Elastic IP** addresses changes the network architecture significantly, introduces security exposure, and does not address **NAT Gateway** charges for **DynamoDB** traffic specifically. It also increases operational complexity.

**C is incorrect:** An **Application Load Balancer** is used to distribute traffic to backend targets and is not a mechanism for routing traffic to **Amazon S3** or **Amazon DynamoDB**. This adds unnecessary cost and complexity.

**D is incorrect:** **VPC Flow Logs** and **AWS Cost Explorer** are monitoring and analytics tools, not architectural solutions that reduce data-processing charges. They provide visibility but do not eliminate **NAT Gateway** costs.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: VPC Endpoints – Eliminate NAT Gateway Costs for S3/DynamoDB

text: A company runs a large fleet of **Amazon EC2** instances inside a **VPC** in the **us-east-1** region. The instances frequently read and write objects to **Amazon S3** and query **Amazon DynamoDB** tables. Currently, all traffic from the instances is routed through a **NAT Gateway** to reach these AWS services, resulting in unexpectedly high monthly data-processing charges. A solutions architect must reduce costs without changing application code or degrading performance. Which action provides the **MOST cost-effective** solution?

options.A: Replace the **NAT Gateway** with a **NAT Instance** using a cost-optimised EC2 instance type to reduce the per-GB data processing fee
options.B: Create **Gateway VPC Endpoints** for **Amazon S3** and **Amazon DynamoDB**, and update the route tables so that traffic to those services no longer passes through the **NAT Gateway**
options.C: Enable **VPC Flow Logs** and use **AWS Cost Explorer** to identify and terminate EC2 instances that generate the most S3 and DynamoDB traffic
options.D: Move the EC2 instances to a public subnet and assign **Elastic IP addresses** so that they can reach **Amazon S3** and **Amazon DynamoDB** directly over the internet

explanation:
**B is correct:** **Gateway VPC Endpoints** for **Amazon S3** and **Amazon DynamoDB** are available at **no additional cost** — there is no hourly charge and no per-GB data-processing fee. Traffic travels entirely over the AWS private network and never touches the **NAT Gateway**, eliminating the NAT Gateway data-processing charge ($0.045 per GB) for all S3 and DynamoDB traffic. Route table entries are updated to point the relevant prefixes at the endpoint rather than the NAT Gateway; no application code changes are required.

**A is incorrect:** Replacing the **NAT Gateway** with a **NAT Instance** can reduce the per-GB charge but introduces significant *operational overhead* (patching, HA configuration, instance cost) and still charges for EC2 instance-hours. It does not eliminate the data-processing cost as effectively as a free Gateway endpoint.

**C is incorrect:** **VPC Flow Logs** and **AWS Cost Explorer** are visibility and analysis tools. They do not reduce data-transfer costs and terminating busy instances would degrade application performance, not optimise cost.

**D is incorrect:** Moving EC2 instances to a public subnet exposes them to the internet, creating a significant *security risk*. Additionally, traffic to S3 and DynamoDB would still traverse the public internet unless a VPC Endpoint is also configured, and public IP assignment does not eliminate data-transfer charges.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: VPC Endpoints – Eliminate NAT Gateway Costs for S3/DynamoDB

text: A company runs a data-processing application on a fleet of **Amazon EC2** instances inside a **private subnet** in a **VPC**. The application frequently reads large datasets from **Amazon S3** and queries **Amazon DynamoDB** tables. Currently, all traffic from the private subnet exits through a **NAT Gateway** to reach these AWS services. The infrastructure team has noticed that **NAT Gateway** data-processing charges now represent a significant portion of the monthly AWS bill. A solutions architect must reduce costs with the **LEAST operational overhead**. Which solution **MOST cost-effectively** addresses the issue?

options.A: Replace the **NAT Gateway** with a **NAT instance** based on a small EC2 instance type to reduce hourly and data-processing charges for all outbound traffic.
options.B: Create a **Gateway VPC Endpoint** for **Amazon S3** and a **Gateway VPC Endpoint** for **Amazon DynamoDB**, and update the **route tables** for the private subnet to route traffic to these services through the endpoints.
options.C: Enable **S3 Transfer Acceleration** on the **S3 bucket** and use **DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX)** to cache DynamoDB queries, reducing the volume of data traversing the **NAT Gateway**.
options.D: Move the **EC2** instances to a public subnet, assign **Elastic IP addresses**, and configure **Security Groups** to restrict inbound access, eliminating the need for a **NAT Gateway** altogether.

explanation:
**B is correct:** **Gateway VPC Endpoints** for **Amazon S3** and **Amazon DynamoDB** are available at **no additional charge**. Traffic between the **VPC** and these services travels entirely over the AWS private network — it never passes through the **NAT Gateway**. By updating the private subnet **route tables** to point S3 and DynamoDB prefixes at the endpoints, the company eliminates all **NAT Gateway** data-processing fees for those services with minimal operational overhead (no new instances to manage, no application code changes).

**A is incorrect:** Replacing the **NAT Gateway** with a **NAT instance** reduces the hourly rate but still incurs data-processing charges for traffic destined for S3 and DynamoDB, and introduces operational overhead (patching, high-availability configuration, instance management).

**C is incorrect:** **S3 Transfer Acceleration** is designed to speed up uploads over long geographic distances and actually adds cost per GB. **DAX** reduces read latency but does not eliminate the traffic path through the **NAT Gateway** — it adds cost rather than removing it.

**D is incorrect:** Placing instances in a public subnet with **Elastic IP addresses** exposes them directly to the internet, creating a significant security risk. This approach is not an acceptable trade-off and does not follow the *principle of least privilege* for network exposure.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: VPC Endpoints – Eliminate NAT Gateway Costs for S3/DynamoDB

text: A company runs a data processing application on **Amazon EC2** instances inside a **private subnet** in a **VPC**. The application reads large volumes of configuration data from **Amazon S3** and writes session state to **Amazon DynamoDB** every few seconds. Currently, all traffic to both services is routed through a **NAT Gateway**, which is generating $4,800 per month in data-processing charges. The security team mandates that traffic to **S3** and **DynamoDB** must never traverse the public internet. A solutions architect must redesign the network path to eliminate the **NAT Gateway** data charges for these two services while fully satisfying the security requirement. The EC2 instances also need to reach other AWS services (e.g., **AWS Systems Manager**, **Amazon CloudWatch**) that do not support **Gateway Endpoints**. Which combination of actions meets ALL requirements at the **MOST cost-effective** level? (Select the **single best answer**.)

options.A: Replace the **NAT Gateway** entirely with a single **Interface VPC Endpoint** for each required AWS service, including **S3**, **DynamoDB**, **SSM**, and **CloudWatch**, and update the private subnet **route table** to remove the NAT Gateway route
options.B: Create a **Gateway VPC Endpoint** for **Amazon S3** and a **Gateway VPC Endpoint** for **Amazon DynamoDB**, add the generated prefix list routes to the private subnet **route table**, and retain the existing **NAT Gateway** only for traffic to services that do not support Gateway Endpoints
options.C: Create a **Gateway VPC Endpoint** for **Amazon S3** and a **Gateway VPC Endpoint** for **Amazon DynamoDB**, then replace the **NAT Gateway** entirely with **Interface VPC Endpoints** for all remaining AWS services, and remove the NAT Gateway to eliminate all NAT costs
options.D: Enable **VPC peering** between the application **VPC** and the AWS-managed service VPCs for **S3** and **DynamoDB**, update the **route tables** to use the peering connections, and keep the **NAT Gateway** for other AWS service traffic

explanation:
**B is correct:** **Gateway VPC Endpoints** for **Amazon S3** and **Amazon DynamoDB** are provided at **no additional charge** — there is no hourly fee and no data-processing fee. Adding their prefix-list routes to the private subnet route table causes all S3 and DynamoDB traffic to flow directly over the AWS private network, eliminating the bulk of the NAT Gateway data charges identified in the scenario. The **NAT Gateway** is retained (at reduced cost) only for services such as **AWS Systems Manager** and **Amazon CloudWatch** that do not support Gateway Endpoints, satisfying the security requirement that S3/DynamoDB traffic never traverses the public internet.

**A is incorrect:** **Interface VPC Endpoints** for **S3** and **DynamoDB** incur an **hourly endpoint charge plus per-GB data-processing fees**, making them *more expensive* than Gateway Endpoints for high-throughput S3/DynamoDB workloads. Using Interface Endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB instead of the free Gateway Endpoints is not cost-optimal.

**C is incorrect:** While replacing the NAT Gateway with Interface VPC Endpoints for remaining services is architecturally valid, completely removing the NAT Gateway and replacing every service with an Interface Endpoint typically results in *higher overall cost* than retaining a NAT Gateway for the small volume of non-S3/DynamoDB traffic, because Interface Endpoints carry both hourly and per-GB fees. The scenario specifies *eliminating NAT charges for S3 and DynamoDB specifically* — not necessarily eliminating all NAT Gateway usage.

**D is incorrect:** **VPC peering** cannot be used to connect a customer VPC to AWS-managed service endpoints for S3 or DynamoDB. AWS service endpoints are not reachable via VPC peering; this is a fundamental architectural constraint.

metadata.subject: Domain 4 – Design Cost-Optimised Architectures
